table_of_contents.title: Composing implicit functions, level sets, and voxels
Deep learning context: datasets, tasks, and the 2D-to-3D bridge
Early 3D deep learning: multi-view CNNs, voxels, and octrees
Learning on points and surfaces: PointNet, metrics, AtlasNet, deep implicits
NeRF, differentiable volume rendering, and Gaussian splatting

units.name: 3D geometry begins with explicit representations
units.description: Introduces why 3D objects are harder to encode than 2D images and motivates point clouds and polygon meshes as foundational explicit representations.
units.summary: The lecture opens by framing 3D vision as a representation problem: unlike images, 3D objects vary widely in scale, geometry, texture, and material, so there is no single analogue of pixels [ts=95s][ts=129s]. Wu organizes geometry representations into explicit and implicit families, then dives into point clouds as unordered sets of 3D coordinates, optionally with normals; oriented points are called surfels, and normals matter for realistic lighting and shading [ts=156s][ts=301s][ts=340s][ts=365s]. He emphasizes that point clouds often come directly from scanners and depth sensors, but can be noisy, unevenly sampled, and missing topology, which makes undersampled regions hard to render and leaves ambiguity about surface connectivity [ts=434s][ts=480s]. Polygon meshes address this by storing both points and their connectivity, making them the dominant representation in graphics and games and enabling operations like subdivision, simplification, and regularization, though their irregular structure historically made them harder to integrate with early CNN-based deep learning [ts=508s][ts=613s][ts=661s].

key_points.text: 3D object geometry is commonly divided into explicit and implicit representations.
A point cloud is an unordered set of 3D coordinates; adding orientation yields surfels.
Surface normals are needed to render point-based geometry realistically under lighting.
Point clouds often come from scanners and depth sensors and can be noisy or require registration.
Point clouds lack topological information, so connectivity ambiguities remain.
Polygon meshes store both vertices and face connectivity, enabling subdivision, simplification, and regularization.

units.name: Parametric surfaces and the explicit–implicit trade-off
units.description: Explains parametric curves and surfaces, then contrasts the strengths of explicit versus implicit representations.
units.summary: Wu next introduces parametric representations, where a lower-dimensional parameter space is mapped into 3D; the key idea is that many shapes have lower intrinsic dimensionality than the ambient space [ts=698s][ts=731s]. He uses a circle and a sphere as examples, then mentions Bézier curves and Bézier surfaces as flexible smooth representations controlled by a small number of control points [ts=760s][ts=783s][ts=798s]. This leads to a broader comparison: explicit representations directly produce points on the surface, which makes sampling easy because one can plug parameters into the function [ts=863s][ts=941s]. However, explicit forms make inside/outside queries difficult, whereas implicit forms define the surface by a condition such as f(x,y,z)=0, making membership-style queries easier and foreshadowing why implicit functions are attractive for later neural rendering methods [ts=981s][ts=1064s][ts=1172s].

key_points.text: Parametric representations map lower-dimensional intrinsic variables into 3D shape geometry.
A circle can be represented parametrically by varying a single variable rather than storing samples.
A sphere in 3D needs two degrees of freedom, typically u and v.
Bézier curves and surfaces use a small number of control points to model smooth geometry.
Explicit representations make sampling surface points easy.
Explicit representations make inside/outside tests hard, motivating implicit geometry.
Implicit geometry represents a surface by a constraint such as f(x,y,z)=0.

units.name: Implicit composition, level sets, and voxelization
units.description: Shows how implicit functions can be composed, blended, discretized as level sets, and finally binarized into voxels.
units.summary: Before switching to deep learning, the lecture extends implicit geometry by showing why it is compositional: unions, intersections, and differences can be implemented by arithmetic or logical operations on function values, which is why CAD systems can assemble complex manufactured parts from simpler pieces [ts=1264s][ts=1292s][ts=1340s]. Signed distance functions add more semantic meaning because the value also encodes distance to the surface, allowing smooth interpolation and blending rather than only hard Boolean composition [ts=1351s][ts=1396s]. Wu then explains level-set thinking: instead of evaluating a closed-form function on demand, one can prequery a dense 3D grid and store values in a volume, effectively turning an implicit field into a nonparametric grid representation while still recovering boundaries from sign changes [ts=1447s][ts=1516s]. If those stored values are binarized to inside/outside occupancy, the result is a voxel grid—a 3D matrix that is easy to understand and later becomes the first deep-learning-friendly 3D representation [ts=1594s][ts=1619s][ts=1647s].

key_points.text: Implicit functions are easy to compose with unions, intersections, and differences.
Distance functions support smooth blending because their values encode distance to the surface.
Level sets precompute implicit values on a dense grid while preserving boundary information through sign changes.
Voxels are binarized occupancy values on a 3D grid, analogous to pixels in 2D.

units.name: Why 3D deep learning needed new data and task formulations
units.description: Places 3D deep learning in historical context, covering why voxels were the first entry point, which datasets enabled progress, and what tasks matter in AI+geometry.
units.summary: Wu situates 3D deep learning historically after the 2D successes of ImageNet-era CNNs, noting that the first instinct was to lift 2D convolutions into 3D by operating on voxel grids because voxels most closely resemble pixels [ts=1679s][ts=1721s][ts=1730s]. He then argues that methods alone were not enough; progress required larger 3D datasets, moving from small benchmarks like the Princeton Shape Benchmark to ShapeNet and ShapeNetCore, and later to Objaverse-scale collections of synthetic assets, while real scanned scene datasets remained much smaller and more expensive to collect [ts=1781s][ts=1852s][ts=1863s][ts=1910s][ts=2120s]. The segment ends by broadening the task space: 3D learning includes generative modeling, conditional reconstruction from images or language, completion and repair, discriminative recognition, and especially joint 2D–3D modeling that exploits abundant 2D data and differentiable rendering to transfer priors from image models into 3D pipelines [ts=2165s][ts=2188s][ts=2231s][ts=2258s].

key_points.text: Early 3D deep learning started by extending 2D convolution into volumetric convolution on voxels.
ShapeNet provided a large-scale 3D object dataset, with ShapeNetCore serving as the commonly used subset.
Objaverse and Objaverse-XL expanded 3D asset scale to roughly millions of models.
3D scene datasets are much smaller than object datasets because scanning real spaces is costly.
Core 3D learning tasks include generation, reconstruction, completion, classification, and joint 2D–3D modeling.

units.name: Early 3D learning methods: render-to-2D, voxels, GANs, and octrees
units.description: Covers the first practical deep models for 3D recognition and generation, beginning with multi-view CNNs and progressing to voxel and octree methods.
units.summary: The first deep-learning workaround for 3D recognition was not truly 3D-native: render the shape from multiple viewpoints, run a 2D CNN on each image, and fuse the features with view pooling, thereby reusing strong ImageNet-pretrained image models [ts=2330s][ts=2354s][ts=2362s][ts=2391s]. Wu notes that this strategy worked well for classification but depended on projection quality and became brittle for noisy or incomplete geometry, motivating more native 3D methods [ts=2445s][ts=2460s][ts=2471s]. Voxel CNNs and 3D GANs then treated shape generation and classification as volumetric learning problems, benefiting from simple 3D convolution and occupancy-style losses but suffering from wasted computation in empty space and coarse resolution [ts=2508s][ts=2550s][ts=2582s][ts=2648s]. Octrees improved efficiency by recursively allocating fine cells near surfaces and coarse cells elsewhere, letting models reach higher effective resolution within the same memory budget [ts=2650s][ts=2710s][ts=2726s].

key_points.text: Multi-view CNNs solve 3D recognition by rendering many views, encoding each with a 2D CNN, and pooling across views.
Multi-view methods leverage large 2D pretrained models but require reliable projection from 3D to 2D.
Voxel-based deep models apply 3D convolution and can support both generation and classification.
3D GANs extend adversarial learning from pixels to voxels and can also support controllable 3D-to-2D generation.
Octrees recursively partition space so high resolution is used mainly near object surfaces.

units.name: Learning directly on point sets and learned surface parameterizations
units.description: Introduces PointNet, point-set loss functions, AtlasNet, and the conceptual move toward deep implicit shape functions.
units.summary: This segment shifts from voxel-centric learning to methods designed around better geometric representations. PointNet is presented as the first major network for point clouds, with the key design constraints that the model must be permutation invariant to point ordering and robust to sampling variation across the surface [ts=2783s][ts=2797s][ts=2854s][ts=2858s]. The core solution is strikingly simple: compute per-point embeddings and aggregate them with a symmetric function such as max or sum, then use the pooled representation for recognition or downstream tasks [ts=2875s][ts=2896s]. For generation, Wu explains that point outputs require set-based distances rather than pixelwise losses, highlighting Chamfer distance and Earth Mover distance as differentiable objectives for comparing unordered point clouds [ts=3022s][ts=3051s]. He then moves to AtlasNet, where an MLP learns a mapping from low-dimensional UV coordinates to 3D space, effectively learning parametric surface patches that yield smoother outputs than voxels or raw point clouds [ts=3118s][ts=3148s][ts=3231s]. The unit closes with the insight that deep networks themselves can serve as implicit functions over x,y,z queries, motivating 2019-style deep occupancy and deep SDF methods [ts=3352s][ts=3386s].

key_points.text: PointNet directly processes point clouds and must be permutation invariant because point sets are unordered.
Point-cloud models should also be sampling invariant so predictions reflect geometry rather than sampling artifacts.
PointNet aggregates per-point embeddings with a symmetric operator such as max or sum.
Chamfer distance compares two point sets by nearest-neighbor matching in both directions.
Earth Mover distance uses a one-to-one bipartite matching between point sets.
AtlasNet learns an MLP mapping from UV coordinates to 3D surface points, using multiple sheets for complex shapes.
Deep implicit functions query x,y,z with a network to predict occupancy or signed distance directly.

units.name: From deep implicits to NeRF and 3D Gaussian splatting
units.description: Builds from deep implicit geometry to neural radiance fields, differentiable volume rendering, image-only supervision, and the efficiency gains of Gaussian splatting.
units.summary: Starting from deep occupancy and deep SDF ideas, Wu generalizes neural implicit functions so a network can answer richer queries than inside/outside, including signed distance, density, color, and radiance [ts=3469s][ts=3504s]. NeRF is introduced as the step that pairs these queryable fields with viewing direction and differentiable volume rendering: the network maps spatial position and camera direction to density and color, and the rendering equation is differentiable enough to learn directly from 2D images rather than requiring 3D supervision [ts=3548s][ts=3561s][ts=3571s][ts=3608s][ts=3627s]. Wu then notes that radiance-field ideas can also be embedded in generative pipelines to obtain more realistic controllable 3D outputs from image supervision [ts=3761s][ts=3800s]. The main weakness of NeRF is efficiency, since rays still sample many empty locations; 3D Gaussian splatting addresses this by representing the scene with sparse Gaussian blobs so computation focuses on occupied regions, giving similar rendering quality but dramatically higher frame rates [ts=3842s][ts=3913s][ts=3958s][ts=3994s][ts=4000s].

key_points.text: Deep implicit methods can predict occupancy, signed distance, density, and appearance for queried 3D points.
NeRF queries a neural field with x, y, z and viewing direction to output density and radiance.
Differentiable volume rendering lets NeRF learn from 2D images by accumulating color and transmittance along rays.
Radiance-field generators can provide controllable 3D generation while training directly from images.
3D Gaussian splatting uses sparse Gaussian blobs so rays sample mainly where matter exists.
Gaussian splatting achieves NeRF-like quality with much higher rendering speed.

units.name: Structure-aware shape representations and emerging programmatic generation
units.description: Highlights representations that capture symmetry, repetition, relations, hierarchies, and recent ideas that use program-like abstractions and language models.
units.summary: The final segment argues that standard representations such as point clouds, meshes, and implicit functions mostly capture geometric detail of local parts, but not higher-order structure like symmetry, repetition, and part relationships [ts=4042s][ts=4084s][ts=4093s]. Wu sketches a progression of structure-aware representations: part sets built from simple primitives, relationship graphs that encode adjacency or support constraints, and hierarchical or hierarchical-graph models that organize scenes and objects across multiple levels such as rooms, furniture, and chair parts [ts=4100s][ts=4135s][ts=4155s][ts=4186s][ts=4226s]. He closes with program-like generation, where repeated structure can be expressed by loops and compositional rules, and notes an emerging research direction in which large language models may emit such structural programs while implicit functions handle fine geometric detail [ts=4243s][ts=4276s][ts=4290s].

key_points.text: Many classic 3D representations capture local geometry but not global regularities like symmetry and repetition.
Part-set representations build objects from simple geometric primitives.
Relationship graphs model constraints between parts such as support, symmetry, or co-location.
Hierarchical graph models represent multilevel part structure and constraints jointly.
Program-like generation can express repeated structure, and large language models may generate such programs.

concepts_kp_local.name: Explicit vs. implicit geometry taxonomy
concepts_kp_local.description: The organizing distinction between representations that directly enumerate or parametrize surface points and representations that define geometry through a function or field.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: This distinction structures the entire lecture and explains why later deep-learning methods inherit different computational trade-offs.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 120s, the slide 'Many Ways to Represent Geometry' visually separates explicit examples such as point clouds and meshes from implicit families such as level sets and distance functions.

concepts_kp_local.name: Point clouds
concepts_kp_local.description: An unordered set of 3D points, optionally with normals, often produced directly by sensors and useful as a flexible but topology-free representation.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Point clouds are a foundational acquisition format and motivate later issues of permutation invariance and point-set losses.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 300s, the 'Point Clouds' slides show sampled surface points and explicitly note that shading requires normals.

concepts_kp_local.name: Polygon meshes
concepts_kp_local.description: A boundary representation that adds connectivity and faces to sampled points, enabling rich geometric operations and practical graphics pipelines.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Meshes are the dominant practical representation in graphics and provide the baseline against which many neural methods are compared.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 540s, slides showing David and Google Earth emphasize how meshes scale from detailed objects to city-scale reconstructions.

concepts_kp_local.name: Parametric curves and surfaces
concepts_kp_local.description: Representations that map a low-dimensional parameter domain such as t or (u,v) into points on a 2D or 3D shape.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Parametric modeling provides the conceptual bridge from classical geometric modeling to learned decoders like AtlasNet.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 740s, the sphere and Bézier slides depict a 2D parameter domain being mapped smoothly into a 3D surface.

concepts_kp_local.name: Implicit functions for geometry
concepts_kp_local.description: A representation in which surface points satisfy a condition like f(x,y,z)=0, enabling inside/outside reasoning and later neural field methods.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Implicit functions are the conceptual precursor to deep occupancy, signed distance fields, NeRFs, and modern neural rendering.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 1070s, the implicit-representation slide colors positive and negative regions around an f=0 contour to show classification by a field.

concepts_kp_local.name: Level sets and voxels
concepts_kp_local.description: Grid-based discretizations of implicit fields, with voxels storing binarized occupancy values on a 3D lattice.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: They explain both classical field-based modeling and why voxels became the initial substrate for 3D convolution.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 1500s, slides on level sets and voxels show a discretized field and its binarized occupancy interpretation.

concepts_kp_local.name: 3D datasets and AI+geometry task space
concepts_kp_local.description: The data and problem formulations that enabled 3D learning, including large object datasets, scanned scene data, and generative/discriminative/multimodal tasks.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: The lecture explicitly ties methodological progress to data availability and task design rather than architecture alone.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 1850s, dataset slides contrast ShapeNet, Objaverse, and ScanNet-style scene collections by scale and modality.

concepts_kp_local.name: Multi-view and voxel-based 3D deep learning
concepts_kp_local.description: Early neural approaches that either render 3D shapes into multiple images for 2D CNNs or directly process voxel grids with 3D convolution.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: These methods define the first generation of 3D deep learning and motivate later moves toward more efficient and geometry-aware representations.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 2360s, Multi-View CNN slides show multiple rendered views passed through shared convnets and pooled into a shape descriptor; later slides show voxel and octree grids.

concepts_kp_local.name: PointNet and point-set invariances
concepts_kp_local.description: The idea that point-cloud networks should respect permutation and sampling invariance, implemented by symmetric aggregation over point features.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: PointNet introduces the canonical inductive bias for unordered 3D point sets and remains a reference design for later point-cloud models.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 2820s, PointNet slides emphasize permutation invariance and illustrate symmetric pooling as the core operation.

concepts_kp_local.name: AtlasNet and deep implicit shape decoders
concepts_kp_local.description: Learned mappings from low-dimensional parameter spaces or x,y,z queries to geometry, covering both patch-based surface decoders and occupancy/SDF-style networks.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: These models synthesize the lecture’s classical geometry discussion with the idea of using neural networks themselves as shape parameterizations.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 3140s, AtlasNet slides show learned UV patches folding into surfaces, followed by deep-level-set slides that depict direct query-based implicit geometry.

concepts_kp_local.name: Neural radiance fields and differentiable volume rendering
concepts_kp_local.description: A neural field that predicts density and color from spatial position and view direction, trained through differentiable rendering from 2D images.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: NeRF is the lecture’s clearest example of how representation choice, rendering, and supervision source combine to reshape modern 3D vision.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 3570s, the NeRF slides visualize querying density and radiance along camera rays and integrating them with volume rendering.

concepts_kp_local.name: 3D Gaussian splatting
concepts_kp_local.description: A sparse radiance-field-style representation that stores extended Gaussian blobs in space to avoid repeatedly sampling empty regions.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It operationalizes the lecture’s repeated theme that better 3D representations often win by avoiding wasted computation in empty space.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 3915s, the Gaussian-splatting slides depict extended 3D blobs floating in space and compare quality and FPS against NeRF.

concepts_kp_local.name: Structure-aware and programmatic shape representations
concepts_kp_local.description: Representations that encode parts, relations, hierarchies, or executable programs so models can capture symmetry, repetition, and semantics beyond raw geometry.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: The topic is brief in this lecture but points to an important frontier where geometry meets compositional structure and language.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Around 4190s, late slides show hierarchical graphs of chair parts and program-oriented abstractions for repeated structure.

unit_kp_map_local.coverage_rationale: The opening taxonomy explicitly divides 3D geometry into explicit and implicit families [ts=156s][ts=179s].
Point clouds occupy the longest portion of the segment, including normals, scanners, noise, and lack of topology [ts=301s][ts=434s][ts=480s].
Meshes are introduced as the connectivity-rich alternative to point clouds and linked to subdivision/simplification workflows [ts=508s][ts=611s][ts=662s].
The unit centers on parametric curves, spheres, and Bézier surfaces as mappings from low-dimensional variables into 3D [ts=698s][ts=783s][ts=798s].
Implicit geometry is introduced via inside/outside tests and the condition f(x,y,z)=0 [ts=981s][ts=1064s][ts=1173s].
Wu explicitly contrasts explicit sampling ease with implicit membership queries in this section [ts=941s][ts=981s][ts=1173s].
The segment extends implicit geometry through composition and signed distance reasoning [ts=1264s][ts=1351s].
Level sets and voxels are derived directly from prequerying implicit functions on a grid [ts=1447s][ts=1518s][ts=1594s].
Voxels are revisited as the first representation that made 3D convolution feel natural [ts=1721s][ts=1730s].
Most of the unit covers dataset scale, scene-data scarcity, and the main generative/discriminative/joint tasks in AI+geometry [ts=1781s][ts=1910s][ts=2165s][ts=2231s].
This segment is explicitly about early multi-view CNNs, voxel CNNs, GANs, and octrees for 3D learning [ts=2330s][ts=2514s][ts=2650s].
The motivation for using 2D pretrained models depends on the earlier dataset imbalance between 2D and 3D [ts=2391s][ts=2424s].
PointNet, permutation invariance, sampling invariance, and symmetric aggregation define the first half of the unit [ts=2783s][ts=2797s][ts=2875s][ts=2920s].
Chamfer/EMD, AtlasNet, and the move to learned implicit queries occupy the remainder of the segment [ts=3022s][ts=3118s][ts=3352s][ts=3386s].
AtlasNet depends on the earlier parametric-surface idea of mapping UV coordinates into 3D [ts=3118s][ts=3150s].
The segment starts by extending deep implicit geometry into richer point queries [ts=3470s][ts=3504s].
NeRF and differentiable volume rendering are the central conceptual contribution of the unit [ts=3548s][ts=3561s][ts=3608s][ts=3627s].
Gaussian splatting is presented as the efficiency-oriented successor to dense NeRF sampling [ts=3913s][ts=3958s][ts=3994s][ts=4000s].
The final segment is wholly devoted to part sets, relation graphs, hierarchies, and program-like structural generation [ts=4100s][ts=4135s][ts=4186s][ts=4243s][ts=4276s].
Meshes and other raw geometry representations are briefly contrasted with higher-order structural abstractions [ts=4084s][ts=4093s].

section_flags.rationale: Foundational definitions of explicit geometry, point clouds, and meshes recur throughout the lecture; strong concept slides but no live demo.
This section introduces parametric functions, explicit-vs-implicit trade-offs, and several formula-based examples (circle, sphere, f(x,y,z)=0).
Signed distance functions, composition, level sets, and voxelization are conceptually dense and support many retrieval-style quiz items.
The segment is mostly historical and contextual, focusing on datasets, bottlenecks, and task taxonomy rather than derivations.
Early 3D deep-learning paradigms are clearly contrasted here, making the section useful for comparison questions and conceptual review.
PointNet invariances, point-set losses, AtlasNet, and deep implicit queries are major method-design ideas with many assessable claims.
NeRF and Gaussian splatting are lecture highlights; the unit mixes field definitions, rendering equations, and efficiency trade-offs.
Brief but forward-looking wrap-up on structure-aware representations and LLM-generated programs; valuable conceptually, but less developed than core units.
